metadata.title: Economics and Computation - Basic Pass-Focused Question Bank
metadata.created_for: Relearning the course from zero and passing the exam
metadata.study_method: Answer every question, read every explanation, then repeat all mistakes until correct twice.
metadata.important_note: This bank targets the central definitions, formulas, theorem statements, and standard examples. It is designed as a pass-focused foundation, not a guarantee of any particular exam result.

questions.prompt: What is the main goal of mechanism design?
choices: Design rules so self-interested agents produce a desirable outcome
questions.answer: Design rules so self-interested agents produce a desirable outcome
questions.explanation: Mechanism design works backward: choose rules that make strategic behavior lead to good outcomes.

questions.prompt: In a direct-revelation mechanism, what does each agent report?
questions.explanation: The report represents the agent’s private information, such as a value or cost.

questions.prompt: What does DSIC mean?
choices: Truth-telling is a dominant strategy for every agent
Truth-telling is best only when others are truthful
questions.answer: Truth-telling is a dominant strategy for every agent
questions.explanation: Under DSIC, reporting truthfully is best no matter what the other agents report.

questions.prompt: In a DSIC mechanism, an agent must know the other agents’ reports before truth-telling becomes optimal.
questions.explanation: Dominant-strategy truthfulness does not depend on knowing the reports of others.

questions.prompt: What does individual rationality usually guarantee?
choices: A truthful participant gets nonnegative utility
The seller always earns positive revenue
questions.answer: A truthful participant gets nonnegative utility
questions.explanation: Individual rationality makes voluntary participation reasonable because truthful utility is at least zero.

questions.prompt: For a bidder with value v who wins and pays p, what is quasilinear utility?
questions.explanation: A winner receives value v and loses payment p, so utility is v-p.

questions.prompt: For a bidder who does not receive the item and pays nothing, utility is usually:
questions.explanation: With no allocation and no payment, quasilinear utility is zero.

questions.prompt: What is social welfare in an allocation problem?
choices: The sum of the values of allocated outcomes
The sum of all payments only
questions.answer: The sum of the values of allocated outcomes
questions.explanation: Welfare measures the total value created by the chosen allocation.

questions.prompt: What does the revelation principle let us focus on?
questions.explanation: If an outcome can be implemented in equilibrium, it can often be represented by a truthful direct mechanism.

questions.prompt: State the utility formula for agent i in a quasilinear mechanism.
questions.explanation: Utility equals the agent’s value for the chosen outcome minus the payment.

questions.prompt: A single-parameter agent has private information described by:
questions.explanation: Examples include one value for winning or one cost per unit of work.

questions.prompt: In a truthful single-parameter mechanism, the allocation rule should be:
choices: Independent of bids in all cases
questions.explanation: Raising a bid should not make a bidder lose when the bidder previously won.

questions.prompt: What is a critical value?
choices: The smallest bid needed to win, holding others fixed
The seller’s reserve in every mechanism
questions.answer: The smallest bid needed to win, holding others fixed
questions.explanation: A winner pays the threshold at which she would just stop winning.

questions.prompt: In a second-price auction, the highest bidder pays:
questions.explanation: The second-highest bid is the winner’s critical value.

questions.prompt: The standard second-price auction is DSIC for a single item.
questions.explanation: Bidding one’s true value is a dominant strategy in a Vickrey auction.

questions.prompt: In a first-price auction, the winner pays:
questions.explanation: This creates an incentive to shade bids, so truth-telling is not generally dominant.

questions.prompt: A first-price auction is generally truthful in dominant strategies.
questions.explanation: Bidders usually benefit from bidding below their values, depending on beliefs about others.

questions.prompt: Why are payments important in truthful mechanism design?
choices: They align incentives with the allocation rule
questions.answer: They align incentives with the allocation rule
questions.explanation: Proper payments make truthful reporting personally optimal.

questions.prompt: If a bidder wins whenever b_i ≥ 8, what is the normalized truthful payment when she wins?
questions.explanation: The critical threshold is 8, so a winner pays 8.

questions.prompt: Give the two basic ingredients of a mechanism.
questions.answer: An allocation rule and a payment rule
questions.explanation: The allocation chooses an outcome; the payment rule specifies how much each agent pays.

questions.prompt: VCG mechanisms choose an outcome that maximizes:
questions.explanation: VCG selects the feasible outcome with the highest sum of reported values.

questions.prompt: The VCG payment of an agent is based on:
choices: The externality imposed on the other agents
questions.answer: The externality imposed on the other agents
questions.explanation: The agent pays for the welfare loss her presence causes to others.

questions.prompt: Why is VCG truthful?
choices: An agent maximizes her utility by making reported welfare match true welfare
questions.answer: An agent maximizes her utility by making reported welfare match true welfare
questions.explanation: The payment removes terms the agent cannot control, so truth aligns utility with total welfare.

questions.prompt: VCG is designed primarily to maximize social welfare.
questions.explanation: The allocation rule maximizes the sum of reported valuations over feasible outcomes.

questions.prompt: If an agent causes no loss to others by participating, her VCG payment is typically:
questions.explanation: No externality means no Clarke pivot payment.

questions.prompt: In a single-item auction, VCG becomes:
questions.explanation: The winner’s externality is the value of the best excluded bidder, i.e. the second-highest bid.

questions.prompt: A multi-parameter valuation means an agent may have:
choices: The same value for every outcome
questions.explanation: The agent’s private type is a vector or function over outcomes.

questions.prompt: Which part of VCG can be computationally difficult?
questions.explanation: If welfare optimization is hard, directly implementing VCG may also be hard.

questions.prompt: VCG always maximizes the seller’s revenue.
questions.explanation: VCG is welfare-maximizing, not generally revenue-maximizing.

questions.prompt: In words, what is a VCG payment?
questions.answer: The harm or externality an agent causes to everyone else
questions.explanation: It compares the best welfare others could get without the agent with their welfare in the chosen outcome with the agent.

questions.prompt: What is the seller trying to maximize in optimal auction design?
choices: The second-highest value in every case
questions.explanation: Revenue-optimal mechanisms use distributional information to maximize expected payments.

questions.prompt: A reserve price means:
choices: The item is not sold below a minimum acceptable bid
questions.answer: The item is not sold below a minimum acceptable bid
questions.explanation: A reserve can increase revenue by withholding the item when bids are too low.

questions.prompt: Myerson’s optimal auction ranks bidders using:
questions.explanation: For regular distributions, allocate to the bidder with the highest nonnegative virtual value.

questions.prompt: For distribution F with density f, the virtual value is:
questions.explanation: This transformation adjusts value by an information-rent term.

questions.prompt: A bidder with negative virtual value should always receive the item in the basic regular optimal auction.
questions.explanation: The seller usually withholds the item when all virtual values are negative.

questions.prompt: What does regularity mean in Myerson’s framework?
choices: All bidders have the same value
questions.explanation: Monotone virtual values support a monotone allocation rule.

questions.prompt: Expected revenue is connected to expected:
questions.explanation: Myerson’s lemma relates expected payments to expected virtual welfare.

questions.prompt: For a single bidder, the optimal posted price balances:
questions.explanation: Expected revenue is price multiplied by the chance the bidder accepts.

questions.prompt: Adding a reserve price can sometimes leave the item unsold.
questions.explanation: Not selling at low bids can increase expected revenue.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between welfare maximization and revenue maximization?
questions.answer: Welfare maximization maximizes total value created; revenue maximization maximizes expected payments to the seller.
questions.explanation: The two objectives can select different allocations, especially when low values have negative virtual values.

questions.prompt: A normal-form game specifies:
questions.explanation: These three ingredients define strategic interaction.

questions.prompt: A pure strategy is:
questions.explanation: The player chooses a single action with probability one.

questions.prompt: A mixed strategy is:
choices: A probability distribution over pure strategies
questions.answer: A probability distribution over pure strategies
questions.explanation: Randomization can make opponents indifferent among actions.

questions.prompt: A best response is a strategy that:
choices: Maximizes a player’s payoff given others’ strategies
Maximizes total welfare regardless of others
questions.answer: Maximizes a player’s payoff given others’ strategies
questions.explanation: Best responses are defined relative to what the other players do.

questions.prompt: A Nash equilibrium is a strategy profile where:
choices: No player can gain by changing alone
Every player gets the same payoff
questions.answer: No player can gain by changing alone
questions.explanation: Each player’s strategy is a best response to the others.

questions.prompt: Every finite game has at least one mixed Nash equilibrium.
questions.explanation: Nash’s theorem guarantees existence in finite games, possibly using mixed strategies.

questions.prompt: Every finite game has a pure Nash equilibrium.
questions.explanation: Some games, such as matching pennies, have no pure equilibrium.

questions.prompt: A dominant strategy is best:
choices: For every possible strategy choice of the others
questions.answer: For every possible strategy choice of the others
questions.explanation: Dominance is stronger than being a best response at one profile.

questions.prompt: A zero-sum game has payoffs whose sum is:
questions.explanation: One player’s gain is exactly the other player’s loss.

questions.prompt: Define a Nash equilibrium in one sentence.
questions.answer: A strategy profile in which no single player can improve her payoff by deviating alone.
questions.explanation: The key words are unilateral deviation: all other strategies are held fixed.

questions.prompt: In selfish routing, each user chooses a path to minimize:
choices: Her own travel time or latency
The number of edges in the entire network
questions.answer: Her own travel time or latency
questions.explanation: Users act selfishly rather than coordinating to minimize total delay.

questions.prompt: A Wardrop equilibrium has the property that:
choices: All used paths have minimum latency
The social optimum is always reached
questions.answer: All used paths have minimum latency
questions.explanation: No infinitesimal user can improve by switching paths.

questions.prompt: The social cost of a routing flow is usually:
choices: The sum of flow times latency over all edges
questions.answer: The sum of flow times latency over all edges
questions.explanation: Total travel time equals Σ_e x_e l_e(x_e).

questions.prompt: The price of anarchy compares:
choices: Worst equilibrium cost with optimal cost
The number of players with the number of edges
questions.answer: Worst equilibrium cost with optimal cost
questions.explanation: PoA measures efficiency loss caused by selfish behavior.

questions.prompt: For a cost-minimization problem, PoA is usually:
questions.explanation: The optimum cannot cost more than the worst equilibrium.

questions.prompt: A price of anarchy equal to 1 means equilibrium is socially optimal.
questions.explanation: The equilibrium and optimal costs coincide.

questions.prompt: In the classic Pigou network, inefficiency arises because users:
choices: Ignore the congestion externality they impose
questions.answer: Ignore the congestion externality they impose
questions.explanation: Each user considers private delay, not the extra delay caused to others.

questions.prompt: A toll can improve routing by:
choices: Making users account for congestion externalities
Forcing everyone onto the slowest path
questions.answer: Making users account for congestion externalities
questions.explanation: Proper marginal-cost tolls align selfish choices with social cost.

questions.prompt: For affine latency functions, the well-known worst-case PoA bound is:
questions.explanation: For nonatomic selfish routing with affine latencies, equilibrium cost is at most 4/3 of optimum.

questions.prompt: Write the basic definition of price of anarchy for a cost problem.
questions.answer: PoA = cost of the worst equilibrium / cost of the social optimum.
questions.explanation: A larger ratio means more inefficiency from selfish behavior.

questions.prompt: A potential function is useful because it tracks:
questions.explanation: In an exact potential game, a unilateral payoff change matches the change in potential.

questions.prompt: In a finite exact potential game, repeated strict better responses must:
choices: Eventually stop at a pure Nash equilibrium
questions.answer: Eventually stop at a pure Nash equilibrium
questions.explanation: The potential strictly improves and there are finitely many profiles.

questions.prompt: Every finite potential game has at least one pure Nash equilibrium.
questions.explanation: A profile maximizing the potential is a pure equilibrium.

questions.prompt: Congestion games are important examples of:
questions.explanation: Rosenthal constructed a potential function for congestion games.

questions.prompt: At a pure Nash equilibrium, every player’s current strategy is:
choices: A best response to the others
questions.answer: A best response to the others
questions.explanation: No unilateral move yields a higher payoff.

questions.prompt: Best-response dynamics means players repeatedly:
questions.explanation: The process updates one player at a time using a best response.

questions.prompt: Best-response dynamics converges in every finite game.
questions.explanation: It can cycle in general games; potential games provide a convergence guarantee for improving moves.

questions.prompt: A local maximum of an exact potential corresponds to:
questions.explanation: No unilateral deviation increases potential, so none improves the deviator’s payoff.

questions.prompt: Which concept is stronger?
choices: Best response at one particular profile
questions.explanation: A dominant strategy is best against every action profile of the others.

questions.prompt: Why does a potential function help prove convergence?
questions.answer: Every improving move raises the potential, and in a finite game it cannot rise forever.
questions.explanation: This rules out cycles of strict improvement.

questions.prompt: External regret compares a learner’s payoff with:
choices: The best fixed action in hindsight
The best action chosen separately every round
questions.answer: The best fixed action in hindsight
questions.explanation: External regret asks how much better one fixed action would have performed over all rounds.

questions.prompt: A no-regret algorithm guarantees average regret:
choices: Goes to zero as time grows
questions.answer: Goes to zero as time grows
questions.explanation: Sublinear total regret implies vanishing regret per round.

questions.prompt: Multiplicative weights changes action weights based on:
questions.explanation: Good-performing actions gain relative weight; bad-performing actions lose it.

questions.prompt: No-regret learning requires the player to know the opponent’s future actions.
questions.explanation: The guarantee is online and does not assume knowledge of the future.

questions.prompt: If every player has low external regret, the empirical distribution of play approaches:
choices: A socially optimal pure strategy always
questions.explanation: No player can gain much by committing in advance to a fixed deviation.

questions.prompt: Regret is called sublinear when total regret R(T) satisfies:
questions.explanation: Sublinear growth makes average regret vanish.

questions.prompt: The learning rate controls:
choices: How aggressively weights react to feedback
questions.answer: How aggressively weights react to feedback
questions.explanation: Too large can be unstable; too small can learn slowly.

questions.prompt: No-regret learning always converges to a single pure Nash equilibrium.
questions.explanation: Time-average play has equilibrium properties, but actual actions may continue to fluctuate.

questions.prompt: One reason no-regret learning is useful is that it:
choices: Works even when the environment is not known in advance
Requires solving every game exactly first
Guarantees maximum welfare in every round
questions.answer: Works even when the environment is not known in advance
questions.explanation: It provides robust online performance against arbitrary sequences.

questions.prompt: What does “no regret” mean informally?
questions.answer: Over time, the learner performs almost as well as the best single fixed action chosen after seeing all rounds.
questions.explanation: The difference per round becomes negligible.

questions.prompt: Mechanism design without money studies settings where:
choices: Transfers or payments cannot be used
questions.answer: Transfers or payments cannot be used
questions.explanation: Examples include voting, school choice, organ exchange, and facility location.

questions.prompt: In voting, a social choice function maps reported preferences to:
questions.explanation: The mechanism selects one candidate or outcome from the alternatives.

questions.prompt: Strategy-proofness without money means:
choices: Truthful preference reporting is a dominant strategy
questions.answer: Truthful preference reporting is a dominant strategy
questions.explanation: No agent can benefit from misreporting, regardless of others’ reports.

questions.prompt: A dictatorship in social choice means:
choices: One agent’s top choice always determines the outcome
questions.answer: One agent’s top choice always determines the outcome
questions.explanation: The dictator’s most preferred alternative is selected regardless of others.

questions.prompt: The Gibbard-Satterthwaite theorem applies, roughly, when there are:
choices: At least three alternatives and unrestricted preferences
questions.answer: At least three alternatives and unrestricted preferences
questions.explanation: A deterministic onto strategy-proof rule must then be dictatorial.

questions.prompt: Gibbard-Satterthwaite shows that every reasonable voting rule can be both non-dictatorial and strategy-proof on unrestricted preferences.
questions.explanation: It is an impossibility theorem: under its assumptions, strategy-proofness forces dictatorship.

questions.prompt: A matching mechanism without money may assign:
questions.explanation: Matching problems allocate indivisible opportunities without monetary transfers.

questions.prompt: In one-dimensional facility location, the median rule is important because it is:
questions.explanation: Choosing a median prevents an agent from moving the facility closer by lying past her true location.

questions.prompt: Randomization can sometimes avoid deterministic social-choice impossibility conclusions.
questions.explanation: Randomized strategy-proof rules can be non-dictatorial, though they face their own characterization results.

questions.prompt: Name two applications of mechanism design without money.
questions.answer: Examples: voting, school choice, kidney exchange, course allocation, facility location, or matching.
questions.explanation: These settings rely on allocation rules rather than monetary transfers.
